essence: David walks the AWB/FliHub integration and finds workflow resume lands on the wrong step (step four resumes at three), the DX/UX visual-highlighter toggle regressed into hiding the developer panel, and the data views (+1 badge, scroll-capturing text areas, nested/flat layouts) need a Playwright-MCP-driven redesign with realistic data.

actions.text: Fix workflow resume so it returns to the saved step instead of an earlier one
Restore DX/UX buttons as pure visual-highlighter toggles that no longer change state or hide the developer panel
Redesign the data views (badge terminology, scrolling, table layout) iterating via Playwright MCP loaded with realistic data

essence: David flags AWB's state labels as meaningless ('end' really means save-on-end, 'pending' means already saved) and questions mixed fonts in developer-only areas, musing that monospace is the property-grid norm, then approves the described fixes.

actions.text: Implement the discussed label-clarity and developer-area font fixes

essence: Reviewing the AWB redesign side-by-side, David judges the end-user top area (video, branding, primary CTA) clear but the developer-experience section visually terrible, wants proper use of color blocks and consistent typography, and instructs the agent to self-validate every change with Playwright MCP instead of pushing blind.

actions.text: Use Playwright MCP to visually validate each UI change before considering it done

essence: David approves the AWB design guidelines for implementation, gives the agent free rein on the top-toolbar hierarchy issues provided changes are revert-ready, and notes the latest color changes are imperceptible.

actions.text: Action the approved design guidelines and top-toolbar hierarchy fixes, keeping them revertable

essence: David probes AWB's multi-step UX: placeholder vs saved file values are ambiguous, step/end/pending states unclear, the project-folder field only populates lazily at step two raising save-behavior questions, the developer panel lacks contrast versus the CTAs (worst on the brown variant), and the UX button wrongly hides the developer area.
David asks whether the AWB screen anywhere surfaces output file names, absolute paths, or open-in-folder/open-in-code capabilities, and invites frontend design ideas to add them.

David reviews the AWB resume/new-record changes architecturally (resume sends data into the existing tab but also opens an unwanted new tab), orders commit+push and process cleanup, and reopens the recurring port-conflict problem that Signal Studio still lacks the fleet-wide fix for.

actions.text: Stop opening a new tab on resume since data populates the existing tab
Add the standard port-conflict handling code that other servers already have

essence: David debugs the deck builder: dragging an Ecamm Live button onto the deck fails, clear-with-confirm doesn't remove items and there's no delete, he demands a headed-Chrome Playwright verification rather than headless automation, wants staging and production data fixed, and untangles confusion over commits that were never pushed.

actions.text: Verify button drag-and-drop onto the deck in a headed Chrome Playwright run
Fix clear/delete so items can actually be removed from the deck
Fix the data in staging and production and push the unpushed commits

essence: Signal Studio's Playwright MCP e2e tests are broken and spraying hundreds of JSON documents across the new prod/staging/dev data directories, staging holds duplicate and corrupted records for one user; David demands full UAT coverage from the existing plan, reset+seed of dev, documentation of the breakage in flood.md, and recovery of the corrupted data from git history.

actions.text: Reset and seed the development data directory and clean up the hundreds of stray JSON files to reach a clean commit
Record in flood.md that MCP Playwright e2e tests are broken and why
Recover the corrupted user's data from git history, accounting for the directory restructure
Run the full UAT plan, not a single concept

essence: A supposed UAT run has ground on for two-plus hours with crashes, compaction and ~10k generated company files while showing no actual screens; David interrogates Playwright e2e vs Playwright MCP, orders the development directory nuked and reseeded, and wants a genuine human-walkthrough UAT plan plus clarity on the FliHub debt-generation handover item.

actions.text: Nuke and clean the development data directory, distinguishing tracked seed files from generated junk
Create an up-to-date human-walkthrough UAT test plan

essence: Housekeeping across repos and skills: David checks what a skill update overwrote, probes how operations.md wires into the provenance system, sets tone guidance for Joy Juice examples (balanced education, don't oversell), and decides the globally-installed Mochaccino skill should be deleted and generalized into AppyStack instead.

actions.text: Delete the global Mochaccino skill installation and generalize it as an AppyStack skill

essence: David challenges an agent that updated 25 files after only asking permission to update a skill — no transparency on what changed, and calling it 'updated' while claiming no baseline exists is contradictory; agents must disclose the scope of their writes.

Long tutoring session where David bootstraps Lars with two labs — a jq-based public jump-system repo (with JList, generator-sourcing bug fixed) and an appydave-brain starter repo (three brains plus the distilled Lisa librarian, installed via npx degit) — then afterwards designs an operations.md provenance-chain document and sets Mochaccino's schema gap-analysis rules.

actions.text: Write a requirements document for reimplementing the jump system as a Claude skill rather than shell tooling
Design a paired operations.provenance document recording the source files and prompts operations.md was built from
Resolve the publish command issue and publish the SupportSignal product from Studio

essence: AppyStack planning: David flags the magenta/purple primary background as a contrast failure, spawns a background task to check AppyStack recipes for a Zod schema and write one linked to the file-CRUD recipe, asks for best-practice-vs-overengineering balance on the architecture question, and approves the plan straight into development.

actions.text: Review AppyStack recipes for a Zod schema and create one tied to the file-CRUD recipe if missing

essence: David aligns Angela on schema-first UX for SupportSignal: Mochaccino must default to working within the database schema and emit gap analyses (not invent structures) when a desired UI doesn't fit; the app gets three separated databases (prod/UAT/dev) with stable primary keys and Zod-style validation; Angela shifts focus from participants (~80% done, cutoff Friday) to moments-that-matter and incident workflows via Agent Workflow Builder; plus AppyStack upgrade queue and Cloudflare rename warnings.

actions.text: Constrain Mochaccino to read and respect the current data schema, producing gap-analysis output for any schema change requests
Firm up production/UAT/development database separation, stable unique-ID primary keys shown in the UI, and an explicit written schema with validation/error-check buttons per database
Shift Angela's focus to moments-that-matter and incident workflow planning grounded in the schema, documented separately from general feedback
Rename the marketing repo to www.supportsignal.com.au and add a Claude MD warning about updating the Cloudflare dashboard
